title: Robux Rain: A Lesson in Async Control Flow
summary: This is a more technical reflection of an older project I wrote that involved distributed-systems/backend flaws in miniature scale.

sections.heading: What is Robux Rain?
sections.body: Robux Rain is a Discord bot written in Python that allows users to input an browser authentication token and start a robux giveaway. Users can configure the length of the giveaway, the prize pool, as well as the claim window. Once giveaway results are announced, winners can claim thier award via DMs.
A lot of this article will involve basic Python and asynchronous code, the latter of which is probably more important so it's definitely good to have a good understanding of it.

sections.heading: Concurrency vs. Parallelism
sections.body:
Python is a unique language in that by default, the Python runtime can only execute bytecode in one thread at a time due to the [global interpreter lock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_interpreter_lock) (GIL). This meant that [until recently](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/free-threading-python.html), true parallelism, executing code in multiple CPU threads wasn't a possibility.

However, as with most other languages, it's possible to write async code, which involves suspending work/tasks on a single thread enabling other work to be done; this is concurrency. It's important to note that these are not in opposition. Sometimes it may be optimal to use both, only one, or neither depending on the scenario

sections.heading: What is Our Bottleneck?
sections.body:
The way I like to think of how you want to make something faster is immediately asking the question: "what's the bottleneck?" And for many of the performance problems we run into, common bottlenecks are CPU and I/O.

Multithreading is good for CPU-bound work as it optimizes hard work by giving you more workers. On the other hand, async is good for I/O bound work because it optimizes waiting. A good visual is imagining a baker preparing icing while his cake is in the oven. He's able to get work done while waiting for the cake to bake without staring at the oven for an hour and a half for the cake to be ready.

sections.heading: Why it Matters
sections.body:
The last two sections are pretty important because when considering Discord bots as well as other applications that request data from some company via some REST API, you begin to realize that more than 70% of the time your app spends running is spent waiting. 

And once you begin to ask questions of efficency, you inevitability ask the question, "how could this process best spend its time waiting?"

Then the answers become a bit more obvious.

sections.heading: A look at Hikari
sections.body: A lot of functionality from many Discord bots come from commands. (I prefer the slash flavor.) [Hikari](https://github.com/hikari-py/hikari), one of many microframeworks for creating Discord bots, takes advantage of [decorators](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_decorators.asp) in Python to turn functions into commands without modifying the function body. Take a look:

The first decorator registers the function as a command. The second, gives it a name (which may be distinct from the function) and a description of what it does for the Discord UI. The last decorator turns it into a slash command like `/version`.

sections.heading: Good Stuff
sections.body: While the code wasn't as clean and as modular as it could be, it was an original idea and the way the code blocks were organized showed a real workflow. There was:

there were also signs that I understood state needed to be tracked, variables like `listen_list`, `counter`, `payload`, `winner_dict`, `new_recipients` etc. were all pointing toward building a state machine even though I didn't lean into that idea.

There was also this cool use of UNIX timestamps that I saw used across Discord that allowed users to refer to a global clock that can be referenced relatively. For example a timestamp corresponding five minutes from now would appear as `5 minutes from now` regardless of timezone. In the code it looked something like:

`"<t:"+ str(int((time.mktime(rain_end.timetuple()))))+ ":R>"`

sections.heading: Why Ownership Matters
sections.body: Now for the meat and potatoes. The biggest design flaw out of the entire project was these couple of lines:

sections.code:
if window_end < datetime.datetime.now():
    run = False
    await event.message.author.send("Sorry, claim window is closed")
    await message.respond("Giveaway Complete!")
    bot.unsubscribe(hikari.DMMessageCreateEvent, get_reply)

sections.body: While there is nothing wrong with a check to see if the giveaway is ended, why its crucial is its position:

The listener that is used for incoming messages owns its OWN timeout. So a check to see if a giveaway ends can only occur after a DM happens. So if the window ends at 5:00 PM, but nobody DMs the bot after 5:00 PM, the bot does not “wake up” and say: "Window is over. Stop Listening". It stays subscribed forever. Expiration should be controlled by a separate task, not by a user dependent incoming message. 

A bettter mental model is:
`Task A: listen for claims`
`Task B: sleep until claim deadline, then close the session`

sections.heading: Are You Listening??
sections.body: One other subtle issue I noticed in the code was that I registered my listener a bit too late. While we can expect some time to pass between DM and response time, we should never underestimate the eagerness for robux. Taking a look at this code:
I immediately noticed right after:

This means that something like this can happen:

`Bot DMs User A`
`User A replies instantly`
`Bot is still DMing User B, User C, User D`
`Listener has not been registered yet`
`User A's reply is missed`

So even before we get to payload bugs, theres a race between telling users to respond and starting to listen for responses. The listener should exist before users are asked to respond, or better, there should be one permanent global DM handler that routes messages to active giveaway sessions.

sections.heading: Nesting
sections.body:
The DM listener is actually also nested in the same command that starts the giveaway which is convenient for capturing giveaway-specifc variables from `/start_rain` but the downside is that everytime `/start_rain` runs, I create a new event listener. So if three giveaways are active you now have three `get_reply` functions listening to every DM.

Imagine an issue arising where:

`User sends one DM`
`Listener for giveaway A sees it`
`Listener for giveaway B sees it`
`Listener for giveaway C sees it`

Each listener checks:
`if event.message.author.id in listen_list:`

And maybe only one responds. But if the same user is in multiple active giveaways, state cleanup fails, or even worse there are active giveaways across different Discord servers, a single DM could trigger multiple handlers which may be catastrophic at scale. A global DM listener would also handle this issue.

sections.heading: A Lesson on Mutable State
sections.body: The way payloads were handled allowed for a lot to go wrong. After verifying the user was a winner:
Later this is ran:

sections.code:
await event.message.author.send("Successfully received username!")
listen_list.remove(event.message.author.id)
counter -= 1
payload["Recipients"].extend(new_recipients)
payout = rbx_request(...)
new_recipients = []
payload["Recipients"] = []

sections.body:
Because there is an `await` between building the recipient and using/resetting the shared payload state we can imagine a scenario where two users DM at almost the same time:

`User A appends recipient A to new_recipients`
`User A awaits send()`

`User B appends recipient B to new_recipients`
`User B awaits send()`

`User A resumes`
`payload["Recipients"].extend(new_recipients)`

Now new_recipients might contain both A and B.

Then A pays out both, resets the list, and B resumes later with state that has already changed.

The core issue is that variables like `new_recipients`, `payload`, `counter` and `listen_list` are shared mutable objects used by multiple calls to `get_reply`

One solution for this that comes to mind is avoiding shared state entirely and building  a fresh payload for each claim.

sections.heading: "Staring At The Oven"
sections.body:
You may remember earlier I mentioned that async is great for I/O bound tasks because it optimizes waiting. This line is where the earlier CPU-bound vs I/O-bound discussion becomes relevant:

` payout = rbx_request("POST",f"https://groups.roblox.com/v1/groups/{message.options.groupid}/payouts",json=payload)`

An API call, a perfect candidate for `await`, yielding back control to the runtime is called in a way that blocks the thread! This means that while the bot is waiting on Roblox to respond:

items: No other Discord events can be processed

sections.body:
This does not necessarily corrupt state by itself, but it makes the bot feel inconsistent and can amplify the possibility of race conditions.

Here, external API calls should ideally be awaited using an async HTTP client, or be moved into another task.

sections.heading: Cookies should NOT be an option
sections.body:
This is kind of an intresting issue because on one hand, the access to a Roblox account through the [ROBLOSECURITY cookie](https://roblox.fandom.com/wiki/.ROBLOSECURITY) is foundational. Without programmatic access to Roblox accounts, there is no Robux Rain. On the other hand, throwing around info possibly more sensitive that a user & password together might give way to security issues.

To give an analogy, we typically don't care about sharing usernames alone, they're public. Passwords are obviously more sensitive, along with a username they represent a key to someone's account( lets use a key and house analogy). Now the security cookie in this context is like an instantaneous teleportation device, placing you in the middle of a house. At least for the key you may have alarms set in place or additional locks (something like 2FA or unrecognized location emails). What are you going to do if someone teleports in the middle of your house unannounced?

Under the hood, the token is bound to a session via browser cookie, so as long as the associated account doesn't log out, the cookie is valid. But typically people don't log out of personal accounts on personal devices so theres even more risk.

Also, imagine a scenario where someone mistypes or forgets to invoke a slash command and sends the cookie out to a channel of hundreds of people. You're now creating a race condition between your ability to log out of your account fast enough and a bad actor's ability to copy paste text into a chrome extension.

There's also the issue of the raw cookie being send across Discord API calls, but maybe thats a lesser worry.

In my mind, an easy fix for this is some type of encryption that the user can  configure beforehand and pass in an encrypted token so everything is handled internally.

sections.heading: Wrap Up
sections.body:
Aside from major design flaws, this project taught me a lot about creating a workflow and processing data. 

Looking back and reading the code, I can tell it was built with the intent to get a minimum viable product working and not a polished, production ready piece of software.

That eagerness led to a lot of wins and a lot of lessons as well, but I think the most important pieces are:

Especially in a dynamically typed language like python, you can't just think about the happy path, you always need to think about what could go wrong and side effects. Ownership is also important although the related timer/dm problem is probably more of a workflow design slip. If the oven bullet is still puzzling, it goes back to thinking about when to take advantage of the async runtime when we can; if theres time to wait, theres time to do soemthing else.

My final thoughts here is that working on a unique project creates unique problems to solve. And those problems are usually pretty fun.
